title: Listening - Part 1
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1 – 6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear. There are two questions for each extract.

extracts.context: You hear two managers, a man and a woman, discussing a recent recruitment process.
extracts.audio_script: Man: So, looking back at the interviews, I must say I'm slightly underwhelmed by the calibre of the candidates we shortlisted. Woman: Really? I thought the last one, Sarah, showed real promise. Her portfolio was impeccable. Man: True, but [1]it's her lack of hands-on experience in high-pressure environments that gives me pause[/1]. We need someone who can hit the ground running, not someone we have to hand-hold for six months. Woman: I see your point, but talent like hers doesn't come around often. If we don't snap her up, a competitor certainly will. Man: Perhaps. But [2]don't you think her salary expectations are a bit ambitious given her background?[/2] Woman: Frankly, if she delivers the results I think she's capable of, she’ll be worth every penny. We can't afford to be penny-wise and pound-foolish here. Man: I suppose. Let's sleep on it and reconvene tomorrow morning to make a final call.

dialogue.speaker: woman

dialogue.speaker: woman

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I suppose. Let's sleep on it and reconvene tomorrow morning to make a final call.

questions.question: What is the man's main reservation about the candidate named Sarah?

options.B: Her inability to work independently immediately.
options.C: Her performance during the actual interview.

questions.explanation: The man mentions that her lack of experience in high-pressure environments 'gives me pause' because they need someone to 'hit the ground running' without 'hand-holding'.

questions.question: The man mentions salary expectations because he believes...

options.B: she is overqualified for the role.
options.C: the pay requested is disproportionate to her experience.

questions.explanation: He asks if the expectations are 'ambitious given her background', implying the pay doesn't match her current level of experience.

extracts.context: You hear two friends, a man and a woman, talking about a new digital detox retreat they attended.
extracts.audio_script: Woman: So, how are you finding the 'real world' after our week in the wilderness? Man: Honestly, it’s a bit of a sensory overload. I [1]never realised how much the constant pinging of notifications was actually fraying my nerves[/1] until they were gone. Woman: I know what you mean. The first two days were torture for me, though. I kept reaching for my pocket every time I saw something worth photographing. Man: It’s addictive, isn't it? But [2]the workshops on mindfulness were a bit of a letdown for me[/2]. I felt they were a bit too 'new-age' and lacked any practical substance. Woman: Oh, I disagree! I found the breathing exercises quite grounding. They've really helped me keep my cool during the morning commute this week. Man: Well, to each their own. I did appreciate the forced silence during meals, though. It made me actually taste the food for once. Woman: Exactly! It’s all about being present, which is something we've clearly lost touch with.

questions.question: What did the man discover about his relationship with technology?

options.A: It was causing him a significant amount of underlying stress.
options.B: He was more productive when using his devices.
options.C: He missed the social connection it provided.

questions.explanation: He says notifications were 'fraying my nerves', a C1 way of describing being stressed or on edge.

questions.question: What is the man's opinion of the mindfulness workshops?

options.C: They were too abstract and lacked utility.

questions.explanation: He describes them as 'new-age' and says they 'lacked any practical substance'.

extracts.context: You hear two university students, a man and a woman, discussing their upcoming thesis deadline.
extracts.audio_script: Man: I’m seriously starting to panic about this Friday. My data analysis is all over the place. Woman: Don't tell me you're still stuck on the methodology section? We've been at this for months! Man: I know, I know. It's just that [1]every time I think I've cracked it, I find a glaring inconsistency in the sample size[/1]. It’s like chasing shadows. Woman: Look, [2]you’re probably overthinking it. Your supervisor even said your initial findings were groundbreaking[/2]. You just need to tidy up the presentation. Man: Easy for you to say! Your draft was finished last week. I feel like I'm just plodding through treacle at this point. Woman: Tell you what, why don't we go to the library tonight? I can look over your stats and see if a fresh pair of eyes helps. Man: That would be a lifesaver, honestly. I’m just worried that if I don't get this right, my whole argument will fall apart like a house of cards. Woman: It won't. You've got the substance; you just need the confidence to back it up.

questions.question: What is the man's current problem with his thesis?

options.A: He has lost all interest in the subject matter.
options.B: He keeps identifying flaws in his research data.
options.C: He hasn't started the methodology section yet.

questions.explanation: He mentions finding 'glaring inconsistencies in the sample size' every time he thinks he has finished.

questions.question: What does the woman suggest about the man's work?

options.A: It is fundamentally flawed and needs a rewrite.
options.B: It requires a more complex argument.
options.C: Its core content is actually very strong.

questions.explanation: She reminds him that the supervisor called it 'groundbreaking' and tells him he has 'the substance', suggesting the quality is high.
